narrative: As a white male applicant with a lower-middle family income background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles like yours from past students. The model estimated a 59.8% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to succeed right away. The primary driver pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your peers—far below the typical student's midpoint around the 5th or 6th decile—which strongly signaled academic struggles that often carry over to bar performance. Your LSAT score of 24, notably lower than the average of about 37 across applicants, was the next biggest factor reinforcing the risk of failure, as lower scores like yours have historically correlated with bar exam challenges. On a more positive note, your family income bracket actually worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward success compared to higher-income peers who sometimes face different pressures. Full-time enrollment status, which is the norm for over 90% of students, didn't sway things much either way, nor did your undergraduate GPA of 3.0, which sits just below the average of 3.21. Overall, your profile stands out from typical admitted students because of those low Year 3 grades and LSAT, which overshadowed strengths like your income level and pulled your risk higher than most. While this decision stings, especially given your dedication to full-time study, it reflects patterns in the data rather than a judgment on your potential. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your Year 3 performance through targeted coursework or tutoring, and reapplying next cycle with those improvements highlighted. You might also explore part-time programs or schools with more flexible bar passage support to build a stronger case. Many students in similar situations have turned things around by addressing these key areas head-on. Keep pushing—your story isn't over yet.
